title: Powers change scale; roots recover it

objectives.statement: Apply integer and rational exponent laws while preserving bases, grouping, and domain restrictions.
Interpret principal roots and rational exponents as inverse power operations over an explicitly declared real domain.
Solve elementary square, cube, and power equations and verify every candidate in the original equation.
Predict how powers and roots change order of magnitude and challenge exact results against that prediction.

scenes.title: Tripling a length does not triple an area
scenes.contentHtml:
Tripling a length does not triple an area

A fictional square detector changes side length from 4 units to 12 units. A report says its active area also triples.

The side ratio is 12/4=312/4=3, but area depends on the square of length:

12242=(124)2=32=9.\frac{12^2}{4^2}=\left(\frac{12}{4}\right)^2=3^2=9.

The report confused linear scaling with quadratic scaling. This lesson asks four questions whenever a power appears: What is the base? What is the exponent acting on? What inverse root is valid in the declared domain? What scale should the result have?

Starting check

  • Is 23⋅242^3\cdot2^4 equal to 272^7 or 474^7?
  • Does x2=25x^2=25 have one real solution or two?
  • Is x2\sqrt{x^2} always equal to xx?

If exponent laws feel memorized but fragile, expand one small integer case into repeated factors before using the symbolic rule.

Source Note
claims claim-power-notation, claim-magnitude-checksources source-nist-sp811-notation, source-nist-estimation

NIST documents power-of-ten notation and the use of magnitude estimates for reasonableness. The detector and scaling diagnosis are original.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every exponent and root is stated in prose and symbols. No task relies on superscript size, color, dragging, or a diagram. Read x3/2x^{3/2} aloud as “x to the three-halves power.”

scenes.title: Read powers as repeated structure
scenes.contentHtml:
Read powers as repeated structure

For positive integer nn, ana^n means nn repeated factors of aa. That definition explains the laws rather than merely naming them:

aman=am+n,aman=am−n (a≠0),(am)n=amn.a^m a^n=a^{m+n},\qquad \frac{a^m}{a^n}=a^{m-n}\ (a\ne0),\qquad (a^m)^n=a^{mn}.

Also (ab)n=anbn(ab)^n=a^n b^n. This rule applies to a product, not a sum: (a+b)2(a+b)^2 requires distribution.

Zero and negative exponents preserve the laws:

a0=1,a−n=1an,a≠0.a^0=1,\qquad a^{-n}=\frac1{a^n},\qquad a\ne0.
Definition
id definition-rational-exponent

For real-number work where the indicated root exists, a1/na^{1/n} denotes the principal nnth root of aa, and am/n=(a1/n)ma^{m/n}=(a^{1/n})^m. For even nn, require a≥0a\ge0; for negative exponents also require a≠0a\ne0.

Thus 272/3=(273)2=927^{2/3}=(\sqrt[3]{27})^2=9. The cube root is defined for negative real inputs, but the real square root is not.

Powers and order of magnitude

If x≈10kx\approx10^k, then xp≈10kpx^p\approx10^{kp}. A square doubles the base-10 exponent; a square root halves it. So 108\sqrt{10^8} should be near 10410^4, not 10810^8.

Source Note
claims claim-power-notation, claim-exponent-laws, claim-magnitude-checksources source-nist-sp811-notation, source-nist-estimation

NIST supports the power-of-ten representation and magnitude-checking role. The laws and rational-exponent derivations are original mathematical inference from repeated multiplication and inverse operations.

scenes.title: Use a root to recover a scale
scenes.contentHtml:
Use a root to recover a scale

A fictional square array contains N=7.29×106N=7.29\times10^6 cells arranged with the same number ss along each side. Find ss.

Worked Example
id worked-example-square-array

1. Model. N=s2N=s^2 with domain s≥0s\ge0 because ss is a count along a side.

2. Estimate scale. Since NN is near 10710^7, ss should be near 103.510^{3.5}, a few thousand.

3. Apply the principal root.

s=7.29×106=7.29106=2.7×103=2700.s=\sqrt{7.29\times10^6}=\sqrt{7.29}\sqrt{10^6}=2.7\times10^3=2700.

4. Verify. (2.7×103)2=7.29×106(2.7\times10^3)^2=7.29\times10^6.

5. Interpret. The algebraic equation s2=Ns^2=N has candidates s=±Ns=\pm\sqrt N, but the declared nonnegative count domain retains only 2700.

Check
id check-root-scale

Why would 2.7×1062.7\times10^6 be impossible before squaring it exactly?

Squaring a million-scale number produces a trillion-scale result, far above 7.29×1067.29\times10^6.

Source Note
claims claim-power-notation, claim-exponent-laws, claim-magnitude-checksources source-nist-sp811-notation, source-nist-estimation

The array, derivation, and domain check are original applications of power notation and magnitude reasoning.

scenes.title: A root can hide a sign decision
scenes.contentHtml:
A root can hide a sign decision

Misconception
id misconception-root-square

It is tempting to cancel the square and root in x2\sqrt{x^2} and write xx. But the principal square root is nonnegative:

x2=|x|.\sqrt{x^2}=|x|.

For x=−4x=-4, the left side is 16=4\sqrt{16}=4, not −4-4.

Likewise, solving x2=16x^2=16 requires x=4x=4 or x=−4x=-4. Applying the principal-root symbol reports 4; solving the equation recovers both signed candidates unless the domain removes one.

This distinction separates an operation from a solution set. Always substitute candidates into the original equation and then apply contextual restrictions.

Source Note
claims claim-exponent-lawssources source-nist-sp811-notation

The sign-boundary explanation is original mathematical synthesis built on conventional exponent notation.

scenes.title: Switch deliberately between power and root
scenes.contentHtml:
Switch deliberately between power and root

1. Combine powers

Simplify x5x−2x^5x^{-2} for x≠0x\ne0.

Feedback: x5−2=x3x^{5-2}=x^3. The common base remains xx.

2. Power of a power

Simplify (3a2)3(3a^2)^3.

Feedback: 33a2⋅3=27a63^3a^{2\cdot3}=27a^6. The outer exponent acts on the entire product.

3. Rational exponent

Evaluate 163/416^{3/4} over the reals.

Feedback: (164)3=23=8(\sqrt[4]{16})^3=2^3=8 using the principal fourth root.

4. Solve and restrict

Solve y3=−125y^3=-125 over the reals.

Feedback: y=−1253=−5y=\sqrt[3]{-125}=-5. Odd roots permit negative real inputs; check (−5)3=−125(-5)^3=-125.

Recovery route

  • Bases changed while multiplying: expand one case and preserve the shared base.
  • Exponents multiplied in a product: distinguish amana^ma^n from (am)n(a^m)^n.
  • Negative exponent made the value negative: rewrite it as a reciprocal.
  • Root lost a candidate: distinguish principal-root evaluation from solving an equation.
  • Exact result has implausible scale: predict the exponent before calculating coefficients.
Source Note
claims claim-power-notation, claim-exponent-laws, claim-magnitude-checksources source-nist-sp811-notation, source-nist-estimation

All practice and recovery content is original.

scenes.title: Audit cubic scaling
scenes.contentHtml:
Audit cubic scaling

A fictional spherical storage design states only that capacity CC is proportional to the cube of a linear scale rr: C=kr3C=kr^3, with k>0k>0.

One design increases rr by a factor of 1.5. Determine the capacity factor. Then find the required rr factor for doubling capacity.

Feedback after a complete attempt

For the first change,

C2C1=(r2r1)3=1.53=3.375.\frac{C_2}{C_1}=\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^3=1.5^3=3.375.

Capacity increases by a factor of 3.375, not 1.5.

For doubling capacity,

2=f3,f=21/3≈1.260.2=f^3,\qquad f=2^{1/3}\approx1.260.

Check: 1.2603≈2.001.260^3\approx2.00. The positive root is selected because the scale factor is positive.

Source Note
claims claim-exponent-laws, claim-magnitude-checksources source-nist-sp811-notation, source-nist-estimation

The scaling law is supplied as a fictional task assumption. All derivations and checks are original.

scenes.title: Reconstruct the scale map
scenes.contentHtml:
Reconstruct the scale map

  1. Identify the full base and grouping.
  2. Choose the exponent law and state nonzero or real-root restrictions.
  3. Predict how the operation changes order of magnitude.
  4. Use a root to invert a power only within the declared domain.
  5. Substitute candidates and interpret signs.

Retrieval

  • Why do exponents add for a product with the same base?
  • What does a negative exponent mean?
  • Why is x2=|x|\sqrt{x^2}=|x|?
  • If a quantity near 101210^{12} is cube-rooted, what scale should result?

Complete assessment.json. If laws are mixed, return to the repeated-factor explanations. If signs fail, revisit the misconception scene. If an exact answer lacks a scale check, redo cubic transfer with the exponent prediction written first.

Source Note
claims claim-power-notation, claim-exponent-laws, claim-magnitude-checksources source-nist-sp811-notation, source-nist-estimation

The synthesis combines conventional power notation and magnitude checking through an original scale-map procedure.

The digest covers discloseable material task instructions, not hidden system or provider instructions.

assessment.title: Exponents, roots, and magnitude mastery gate

items.prompt: Simplify (x³y⁻²)²/(xy⁻¹) for nonzero x and y. Give a form with positive exponents.

answer.reasoning: The numerator is x⁶y⁻⁴; subtract denominator exponents to obtain x⁵y⁻³, then write y⁻³ as 1/y³.
acceptableResponses: x^5 y^-3 with the stated nonzero domain
commonErrors: Applying the outer square only to x
Treating a negative exponent as a negative coefficient

items.prompt: Evaluate 81^(3/4) over the reals using the principal fourth root.

answer.reasoning: The principal fourth root of 81 is 3, and 3³ = 27.
commonErrors: Using both signs for the principal root

items.prompt: Solve (x - 1)² = 36 over the reals and verify both candidates.

answer.correct: x - 1 = ±6, so x = 7 or x = -5. Both verify because 6² = 36 and (-6)² = 36.
answer.reasoning: Solving a square equation requires both signed square-root branches before isolating x.
acceptableResponses: x = -5, 7 with substitution into the original
commonErrors: Writing x = ±5 after applying the shift incorrectly

items.prompt: Which statement is valid for every real x?

answer.summary: The principal square root of x² is |x|.
answer.reasoning: The principal square root is nonnegative; absolute value returns the nonnegative magnitude for either sign of x.
commonErrors: Cancelling the root and square without considering x < 0
Confusing a principal-root value with two solutions to an equation

items.prompt: Estimate √(4.9 × 10¹¹) to one significant digit and explain the predicted order of magnitude before calculating.

answer.correct: About 7 × 10⁵. Because 10¹¹ lies between 10¹⁰ and 10¹², its square root lies between 10⁵ and 10⁶; rewriting 4.9 × 10¹¹ as 49 × 10¹⁰ gives √49 × 10⁵ = 7 × 10⁵.
answer.reasoning: The exponent is made even before taking the root, and the coefficient 49 has exact root 7.
commonErrors: Halving 11 and writing a noninteger exponent without handling the coefficient
Returning a value on the 10¹¹ scale

items.prompt: A fictional capacity follows C = kr³ with k > 0 and r > 0. Design B has 2.4 times the r of design A. Find C_B/C_A. Then determine the r factor needed to make capacity 8 times larger. Show exponent reasoning, a magnitude prediction, and reverse checks.

answer.correct: C_B/C_A = (2.4)³ = 13.824, plausibly a little above 2³ = 8 and below 3³ = 27. For an eightfold capacity, f³ = 8, so f = 2. Checks: 2.4³ = 13.824 and 2³ = 8.
answer.summary: The capacity factor is 13.824; an eightfold capacity requires a linear factor of 2.
answer.reasoning: The proportionality constant cancels in the ratio, leaving the cube of the linear factor; the inverse scale is the positive cube root.
acceptableResponses: Equivalent exact reasoning with positive-domain interpretation and both checks
commonErrors: Using a linear factor for capacity
Selecting a negative cube-root factor despite r > 0

criteria.description: Applies the outer power and quotient laws correctly.
Reports x⁵/y³ with required nonzero domain.
Uses the principal fourth root and obtains 27.
Identifies absolute value as the valid all-real relation.
Uses the positive cube root to obtain factor 2.
Performs both reverse checks and respects r > 0.

claims.statement: Power-of-ten notation separates a coefficient from an exponent that records scale, supporting explicit comparison of orders of magnitude.
claims.scope: Base-10 numerical scale comparisons used in the lesson.
claims.uncertainty: Software display conventions can vary and do not replace algebraic interpretation.

claims.statement: The product, quotient, and power rules for exponents follow from repeated multiplication and extend consistently to zero, negative, and rational exponents when required domain restrictions are preserved.
claims.scope: Real-number elementary exponentiation with nonzero divisors and real roots where defined.
claims.uncertainty: General exponentiation over complex numbers and branch choices are outside scope.

claims.statement: An approximate order of magnitude can predict the scale of a powered or rooted result and help identify implausible exact outputs.
claims.scope: Routine positive-magnitude calculations where powers of ten provide a useful scale estimate.
claims.uncertainty: A plausible magnitude does not prove an exact result or model is correct.

terms.definition: The quantity acted on by an exponent.
An exponent expressible as m/n; over a valid real domain it combines an nth root with an mth power.
The designated nonnegative even root, or the unique real odd root, represented by a radical symbol.
A power-of-ten description of a quantity's approximate scale.
A multiplicative factor comparing a new linear measure or quantity with its reference value.
The set of input or candidate values for which an expression and task are defined.

Originality, evidence, and rights

The scale-map structure, detector opening, square-array example, sign misconception, cubic transfer, all values, feedback, recovery routes, and assessments are original. All settings are fictional. NIST sources are used only for facts about power-of-ten notation and estimation's role in magnitude/reasonableness checks. No source expression, example, table, or image is copied or adapted.

NIST rights, technical-series licensing, and public robots routes were checked before access on 2026-07-22. No agent restriction, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found. No third-party assets, datasets, personal data, or patient material are included.

This mathematical lesson provides no clinical advice and makes no claim of review, approval, publication, measured effectiveness, credit, or competence.
